title: Beyond the Hype: A Developer’s Honest Take on AI-Powered Coding
author: Tosif khan
text:
Beyond the Hype: A Developer’s Honest Take on AI-Powered Coding
— just real experiences and hard-earned insights from the trenches.
After five months of coding with AI-powered editors like Augment Code, Windsurf, and Cursor, I’ve discovered that “vibe coding” or AI-Powered Coding isn’t just about letting AI write your code. It’s about fundamentally rethinking how we approach software development. Here’s everything that made my workflow genuinely more productive.
Learn to Design — LLMs can code, Treat them Like Junior Engineers, Not Principal Architects
The biggest mistake I noticed is that the developers sometimes hand over the architectural decisions to AI. These models are incredibly powerful at implementation, but they’re terrible at high-level design decisions.
Instead of asking “build me a user authentication system,” I now start with the architecture:
- Define patterns first: Before writing any code, I establish the low-level design (LLD). What are the interfaces? How do components interact? What are the boundaries? The Errors ? etc.
- Set boundaries: Clear architectural constraints help the AI generate cleaner, more modular code that actually fits your system.
- Iterate on patterns: When the AI generates something that doesn’t match your design, you don’t necessarily need to accept it. Try to understand the reasoning behind the generation, Provide feedback until it aligns with your architectural vision.
This approach has transformed my codebase from a collection of AI-generated functions into coherent, maintainable systems.
Tests Became My Secret Weapon
I’ll be honest — I wasn’t always a test-first developer. But working with AI changed that completely.
Here’s why: AI can break things in ways you’d never expect. Many times we find ourselves in a situation when one small change can cascade into three other failures. Tests catch these issues before they become debugging nightmares.
My workflow now looks like this: Plan → Write Tests → Generate Code → Execute → Review → Repeat
This cycle has saved me countless hours of debugging and refactoring. The AI generates code that passes your tests, which means it’s more likely to actually work in your system.
Conciseness is the key
Vague prompts are the enemy of good AI-generated code. I learned this the hard way.
❌ A Potentially Bad prompt: “Give me top 10 trending crypto coins”
Result: It can be anything, a list of coins or some javascript or python code, but more like a Spaghetti with un-related modules and zero abstraction, each next prompt will lead you to a different route.
✅ An Improved Version: “Build me a script that fetches the top 10 trending crypto coins using Python’s requests library. Define Pydantic models for request/response validation and return data in dict format with current prices. keep the code consistent and modular, Keep the API URL, Top N Coins, Trending By their properties parameterized, Implement the interfaces first before moving to implementation. Write unit test cases and verbose logging.”
Result: Clean, well-structured code that follows best practices
The difference is? Context and constraints. When you’re specific about:
- Technologies to use
- Data structures expected
- Validation requirements
- Output format
…the AI generates code that actually fits your needs. This can be taken into the next level by having a “Planning Phase” before starting the implementation.
Break Down Complex Problems
Large, complex problems make AI models lean heavily on their “generative” side, which can derail your vision completely. Instead, I’ve learned to:
- Identify independent sub-problems
- Prioritize and solve them individually
- Provide deep context for complex pieces
For debugging, instead of “why is this API returning XYZ?”, I will provide:
- The exact request body
- Relevant function code, which is transforming or preparing the request.
- Database calls (if involved)
- Expected vs actual behavior
This level of detail leads to meaningful solutions instead of generic troubleshooting.
Context Documents Are Your Foundation
Every project now starts with a comprehensive context document. There’s no perfect template, but mine typically includes:
- Core MVP description: What does this app actually do?
- Technology stack: With brief justifications for choices
- Requirements: Essentially your PRD
- Implementation timeline: Order of feature delivery
I usually write it down by myself, then polish it with my LLMs, where I ask questions about my Understandings / Approaches / Possible Caveats/ How Well it fits with the Bigger Picture.
For Cursor users, leverage .cursorignore and .cursorrules files religiously. Before tackling any new problem, ask yourself:
- Do my cursor rules align with this problem? Do I need another rule to handle this case ?
- Are my requirements clearly mentioned ?
- Am I ignoring the right files for this module?
Pro tip: Use Cursor’s / commands to auto-generate these configuration files.
MCPs Are Your Superpower
Model Context Protocols (MCPs) have become game-changers. Almost every mainstream tool now provides their own MCPs, allowing seamless integration between your AI editor and external services.
This isn’t just about convenience — it’s about maintaining context across your entire development ecosystem.
Popular MCPs Worth Exploring:
- GitHub MCP: Direct repository access and PR management
- Slack MCP: Team communication integration
- Database MCPs: Direct database querying and schema access
- API Documentation MCPs: Automatic endpoint discovery
My Personal Favorites:
- Sequential thinking: to make the model think step by step.
- Playwright-mcp: for browser automation.
- Aws-documentation: to access the latest docs and best practices of aws.
Git: Your Safety Net
When working with AI, things can go sideways fast. I’ve adopted a checkpoint-heavy Git workflow:
- Completed a feature? Commit.
- Solved a tricky bug? Commit.
- Major refactor works? Commit.
This granular approach has saved me from countless “how did I break everything?” moments.
Pro Tip: Use Linting / Formatting or even Pre-Commit Hooks for extra code keeping.
The Reality Check: AI’s Limitations
Despite following all these practices, The current models is still having limitations:
The Unhinged Factor: Even with perfect instructions, models sometimes introduce unnecessary changes or write overly complex solutions to simple problems.
Innovation Gap: AI doesn’t innovate (at least as of now) — it recombines existing patterns. For truly novel solutions, you’re still the creative force.
The Yes-Man Problem: AI models are almost comically agreeable. They’ll praise your “excellent architecture” even when you’re completely wrong. Don’t let this fool you into thinking you’re infallible.
Prompting Not Works Always: At the end it boils down to the Model Parameters, The architecture, Support of Tooling(Models like Deepseek, and few small models does not supports MCP tools),
Cognitive Toll: LLMs might make you think less and generate more, which isn’t always productive. Sometimes the generated solution is not the greatest, but since you haven’t coded it, A high chance, that new Ideas will not cross your mind. And even if you are convinced that its good code, then you might become the bottleneck, since now you need to catch-up with the speed of generation.
The Bottom Line
Five months in, I’m convinced that AI-powered coding isn’t about replacing human judgment — it’s about amplifying it. The developers who succeed with these tools are those who maintain strong architectural vision while leveraging AI for implementation speed. Yet this is still evolving and we don’t where it will take us next.
The key insight? You’re still the architect. The AI is just a very fast, very capable junior engineer who needs clear direction to do their best work.
What’s your experience with AI-powered development? I’d love to hear about your workflows and lessons learned in the comments.
